name: Cruciferous vegetables

book_claims.claim: Linked with reduced risk of several cancers including prostate, bladder and lung
book_claims.mechanism: Contain isothiocyanates, indol-3-carbinol and sulforaphane which trigger detoxifying enzymes

book_claims.claim: Sulforaphane triggers detoxifying enzymes protecting against colon and skin cancer, and in prevention of type 2 diabetes and autism
book_claims.mechanism: Sulforaphane activates phase 2 detoxification enzymes via Nrf2 pathway

book_claims.claim: Indole-3-carbinol is a powerful inhibitor of cancer-promoting enzyme WWP1, freeing tumour suppressor PTEN
book_claims.mechanism: Indole-3-carbinol inhibits WWP1 which normally blocks PTEN. PTEN prevents tumour growth. Mice with surplus PTEN live longer.
book_claims.study_ref: Published in Science journal. Refs 51, 52 in book

supplementary_research.finding: Sulforaphane from cruciferous vegetables activates Nrf2-dependent antioxidant response, reduces markers of inflammation (NF-kB), and shows consistent evidence for cancer risk reduction in observational studies. Evidence is strongest for bladder and lung cancer.

Meta-analysis of 62 studies found cruciferous vegetable consumption inversely associated with risk of cancer of the stomach, colon, rectum, and lung (RR range 0.78-0.84). Dose-response analysis showed significant risk reduction with higher intake.

Meta-analysis of prospective studies found that higher cruciferous vegetable intake was associated with significantly reduced total mortality (HR 0.78, 95% CI 0.70-0.87) and cardiovascular disease mortality.

Sulforaphane from broccoli sprout extract improved fasting glucose and reduced HbA1c in type 2 diabetes patients in a randomized controlled trial, supporting the Nrf2-mediated mechanism for metabolic benefits.
Indole-3-carbinol inactivates the WWP1 ubiquitin ligase, reactivating the PTEN tumour suppressor in cancer-prone mice. This represents a diet-derived small molecule that restores PTEN function.

culinary_pairings.source: Traditional; also activates myrosinase for sulforaphane conversion
Acid balances bitterness and brightens flavor
Umami complements bitter/sulfurous notes (stir-fried bok choy, gai lan)
Classic pairing for stir-fried Chinese broccoli and bok choy
Fat aids absorption of fat-soluble nutrients; roasted broccoli and cauliflower
Heat contrasts bitterness (broccoli rabe, stir-fried greens)
Umami and salt balance bitterness in roasted cauliflower and Brussels sprouts
Nutty richness pairs with blanched or stir-fried greens
Smoke and fat complement roasted Brussels sprouts

seasonality: Cool-season crops; peak autumn through early spring. Broccoli and cauliflower peak October-April. Brussels sprouts sweeten after frost (November-February). Kale and cabbage available year-round but best in cooler months.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: 40-170 mg glucoraphanin in broccoli (varies by variety)
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Requires myrosinase enzyme for conversion. Cooking destroys myrosinase; adding mustard seed restores conversion. Broccoli sprouts contain 10-100x more glucoraphanin than mature heads.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: 30-60 mg in raw broccoli and Brussels sprouts
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Formed from glucobrassicin during chewing/chopping. Acid-catalyzed in stomach to form DIM (3,3'-diindolylmethane), the primary active metabolite.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: 89 mg in raw broccoli, 48 mg in raw cabbage
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Highly bioavailable but heat-labile. Steaming preserves more than boiling.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: 101 mcg in raw broccoli, 141 mcg in kale
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Fat-soluble; consume with olive oil or other fat for enhanced absorption.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: 2.6 g in broccoli, 3.8 g in Brussels sprouts
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Mix of soluble and insoluble fiber; supports gut microbiome diversity.

synergies.description: Mustard contains myrosinase enzyme which converts glucoraphanin into the cancer-suppressor isothiocyanate
